data.content:
Implementation plan:

- Add a new `loom update` subcommand in `crates/loom-cli/src/main.rs`, wire `crates/loom-cli/src/commands/mod.rs`, and implement the behavior in a new `crates/loom-cli/src/commands/update.rs` module.
- Make target selection explicit instead of reusing `install.sh` as-is. The implementation must only recognize the release artifacts that actually ship today: `x86_64-unknown-linux-musl`, `aarch64-unknown-linux-musl`, and `aarch64-apple-darwin`. Return a clear unsupported-platform error for `x86_64-apple-darwin` and any other unmapped host.
- Define the update UX up front: `loom update` replaces the currently running installed binary when a newer release exists; `loom update --check` reports whether an update is available without mutating anything; `loom update --force` reinstalls the resolved version even when it matches the current version. If the executable path is not writable or does not look like an installed binary location, fail with an actionable error instead of silently downloading.
- Use GitHub release asset redirect URLs instead of the public Releases API for `latest` resolution so users do not burn API rate limit. For latest downloads/checksums, hit `https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/releases/latest/download/...`, follow redirects, and infer the resolved version from the final redirected asset URL. Keep explicit-version installs/updates on the tag-specific `/releases/download/<tag>/...` path.
- Mirror the installer flow in Rust: download the tarball and `loom-checksums.txt`, verify the selected archive checksum, unpack into a temp dir, and atomically replace the current executable. This will require adding small HTTP/archive/checksum dependencies to `crates/loom-cli/Cargo.toml`; keep them focused and cross-platform.
- Tighten `install.sh` target handling in the same change so the shell installer and `loom update` agree on supported platforms and error messages. The shell script already uses the smart redirect endpoint for `latest`; preserve that behavior while removing the incorrect implication that any Darwin x86_64 build exists.
- Update launch-facing docs in the same change: `README.md` and/or `docs/getting-started.md` for the new `loom update` command and expected behavior, plus `docs/releasing.md` for the supported release matrix and the installer/update reliance on redirect-based release assets.

Test strategy:

- Add focused unit tests for target mapping, URL construction, redirected-version parsing, and unsupported-platform errors.
- Add CLI integration coverage in `crates/loom-cli/tests/cli_integration.rs` (or a dedicated update integration test file) for `loom update --check`, no-op when already current, `--force`, and failure on unsupported targets.
- Structure network/download code behind a small abstraction or injectable base URL so tests can run against a local stub server rather than GitHub.
- Re-run `cargo test`, and because the CLI surface changes, also run `cargo fmt --check` and `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` during implementation.

Risks / open points to resolve during implementation:

- Replacing the currently running executable must be done with a temp file + rename flow that works on supported Unix hosts.
- Version comparison must distinguish current `CARGO_PKG_VERSION` from the resolved release tag format (likely `v<version>`).
- The command should document whether only self-updates are supported or whether a non-installed dev binary invocation is intentionally refused; the safer initial scope is to support self-updating only installed binaries.
